results.possibleIdentity: Waldemar A. Craig (born Wsevolode Grünberg) was an aeronautical engineer active in the mid-to-late 20th century, notably during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is recognized for developing the "Grunberg system" (or Grunberg scheme) for hydrofoil craft, a design that utilizes a submerged rear foil and frontal stabilizers (skis) that ride on the water's surface to provide automatic stability and wave-sensing. Craig conducted his early pioneering work in France starting in the mid-1930s before emigrating to the United States and settling in New Jersey. Based in Union City, New Jersey, he was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, where he was listed as a technical member with expertise in aeronautics. His historical records also include correspondence with Albert Einstein regarding complex personal and inheritance matters, which are preserved in various autograph and historical archives.
